page.title: What Is Medicare IRMAA? {year} Income Surcharge Explained | Vernal Medicare
page.heading: Medicare IRMAA: The Income-Related Surcharge, Explained
page.description: IRMAA is an income-based surcharge added to Medicare Part B and Part D. Learn how the {lookbackYear}-income lookback works, who pays, and how to appeal — from a local Vernal agent.
page.summary: IRMAA (the Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount) is an extra charge added to your Medicare Part B and Part D premiums when your income is above a threshold — {irmaaSingle} for a single filer or {irmaaMarried} for a married couple in {year}. Social Security uses your MAGI from {lookbackYear} to decide. If your income has since dropped because of a life-changing event, you can appeal.

faqs.question: What income does IRMAA use?
faqs.answer: Your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) — adjusted gross income plus tax-exempt interest — from your tax return two years ago. For {year}, that's your {lookbackYear} return.

faqs.question: Does IRMAA go away if my income drops?
faqs.answer: Yes. IRMAA is recalculated every year from your latest return, so a lower income eventually lowers it automatically. If the drop came from a qualifying life-changing event, you can appeal now instead of waiting two years.

faqs.question: Is IRMAA the same for Part B and Part D?
faqs.answer: The income tiers are the same, but the dollar amounts differ. Part B IRMAA is added to your Part B premium; Part D IRMAA is billed separately by Social Security, on top of whatever your drug plan charges.

sources.title: Medicare premiums: Rules for higher-income beneficiaries

nextSteps.when: If your income is near a threshold,
nextSteps.action: run the numbers in our IRMAA calculator, then call us free to plan around it.

meta.ogTitle: Medicare IRMAA: The Income Surcharge, Explained
meta.ogDescription: Who pays IRMAA in {year}, how the {lookbackYear}-income lookback works, and how to appeal.

body.introHtml: IRMAA — the Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount — is an extra charge Social Security adds to your Medicare Part B and Part D premiums when your income is above a set threshold. Most people never pay it. But if you do, it can add hundreds of dollars a month, so it's worth understanding before it shows up.
body.howHtml: Every year, Social Security looks at your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) — your adjusted gross income plus any tax-exempt interest — from two years back. For {year}, that means your {lookbackYear} tax return. If that number is above {irmaaSingle} (single) or {irmaaMarried} (married filing jointly), you fall into an IRMAA tier and pay a surcharge on top of the standard premium.
body.surchargeIntro: The surcharge applies to both parts:
partItemsHtml: Part B IRMAA is added directly to your Part B premium.
Part D IRMAA is billed separately by Social Security — on top of whatever your drug plan already charges.
body.seeHtml: See the full {year} IRMAA brackets for the exact dollar amounts at each income tier, or estimate your surcharge from your income.
body.h2Lookback: The Two-Year Lookback Is the Catch
body.lookbackHtml: Because IRMAA uses income from two years ago, a one-time spike — selling a home, a large Roth conversion, cashing out investments — can trigger a surcharge in a year when your income is actually much lower. The good news: IRMAA is recalculated every year, so it corrects itself as newer returns come in.
body.h2Appeal: If Your Income Dropped, You Can Appeal
body.appealHtml: You don't always have to wait two years. If your income fell because of a qualifying life-changing event — retirement, the death of a spouse, divorce, and more — you can appeal your IRMAA with Form SSA-44 and have Social Security use your current income instead.
nextItemsHtml: {year} IRMAA brackets — the exact surcharge at every income tier.
IRMAA calculator — estimate your Part B and Part D surcharge.

body.outroHtml: IRMAA planning is one of the most valuable things a good agent does. If you're near a threshold, call us free — a small move can save a full tier.
